institution: Sector-wide typology - Indian retail digital payments
category: Social engineering - fake bank customer-support / remote-access scam
risk_signals: Victim calls a 'helpline' number found via a search engine or ad rather than the official site
'Support agent' asks the victim to install a remote-access/screen-sharing app (AnyDesk, TeamViewer)
Guidance to make a 'test' transaction or read out OTPs to 'resolve' an issue
analyst_reasoning: Fraudsters seed fake support numbers online; once the victim calls, they gain trust as 'the bank' and use remote-access tools to watch the screen and drive transactions, or coax OTPs directly. The install of a screen-sharing app is the decisive escalation.
investigation_steps: Identify how the victim obtained the number (search ad, SMS, fake site)
Check the device for recently installed remote-access applications and their session logs
Correlate the remote session window with the unauthorised transactions
decision: Terminate remote sessions, uninstall the remote-access app, freeze the account, and reset credentials from a trusted device.
outcome: With screen control the fraudster can complete transfers directly; loss ends when the remote session is cut.
lessons_learned: Genuine bank support never asks you to install a screen-sharing app
Helpline numbers must come from the official bank site, not search results
Remote-access installation during a 'support' call is an immediate stop signal
